summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a 'gap' in a distribution?

A gap is an empty interval on a histogram where there are no data values. Gaps can suggest separate groups within the data or unusual patterns.

What is a 'cluster' in a distribution?

A cluster is a group of data values that are close together, appearing as a bump or peak in a histogram. Multiple clusters may indicate subgroups.

What is an 'unusual feature' in a distribution?

Unusual features include outliers, gaps, clusters, or unexpected patterns that stand out from the overall shape of the distribution and deserve special mention.

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

An outlier appears as a bar (or small bars) that is isolated from the main body of the histogram, with a visible gap separating it from the other bars.

Dinner tips have a higher center ($8-$15 vs $3-$5), a greater spread (wider range of values), and are right-skewed due to the $50+ outliers, while lunch tips are more symmetric.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A restaurant manager compares lunch versus dinner tips. Lunch tips cluster around $3-$5 with no outliers, while dinner tips cluster around $8-$15 with several $50+ outliers. How do the distributions differ?

Dinner tips have a higher center ($8-$15 vs $3-$5), a greater spread (wider range of values), and are right-skewed due to the $50+ outliers, while lunch tips are more symmetric.

Example: An athletic trainer records vertical jump heights (in inches) for two teams. Team A: mean 20, SD 2. Team B: mean 22, SD 6. Which team is more consistent, and which has a higher average?

Team A has a smaller standard deviation (2 vs 6), meaning its jump heights are more consistent. Team B has a higher mean (22 vs 20), meaning a higher average jump.

Example: A school compares math test scores between Period 1 and Period 3. Period 1 has a symmetric distribution centered at 80 with an IQR of 10. Period 3 has a left-skewed distribution centered at 85 with an IQR of 20. What does this tell us?

Period 3 has a higher center (85 vs 80) but a larger IQR (20 vs 10), indicating more variability. The left skew suggests some students scored much lower than the rest.

Example: A health club tracks the ages of its members. The histogram has two peaks: one around age 25 and another around age 55. How should you describe this distribution?

Two distinct peaks make this a bimodal distribution. The peaks at 25 and 55 suggest two main groups of gym members: young adults and older adults.

Example: A psychologist compares anxiety scores for two groups: therapy patients and a control group. The therapy group has a symmetric distribution centered at 35 with SD 5. The control group is right-skewed with a median of 55 and IQR of 15. What conclusions can be drawn?

The therapy group has a lower center (35 vs 55) and smaller spread (SD 5 vs IQR 15), suggesting lower and more consistent anxiety levels compared to the control group.

summary_sections.title: Special Topics
summary_sections.content_html:
Important Concepts

For a right-skewed distribution, which summary statistics best describe center and spread

For skewed distributions, the median and IQR are preferred because they are resistant to the outliers and extreme values that cause the skewness.

Which feature of a distribution describes its overall pattern (symmetric, skewed, etc.)

Shape describes the overall visual pattern of the distribution, including whether it is symmetric, left-skewed, right-skewed, uniform, or bimodal.

A school compares math test scores between Period 1 and Period 3. Period 1 has a symmetric distribution centered at 80 with an IQR of 10. Period 3 has a left-skewed distribution centered at 85 with an IQR of 20. What does this tell us

Period 3 has a higher center (85 vs 80) but a larger IQR (20 vs 10), indicating more variability. The left skew suggests some students scored much lower than the rest.

A manufacturing plant tracks daily output. Plant A produces a symmetric distribution with mean 500 units and SD 10. Plant B produces a right-skewed distribution with median 480 units and IQR 50. Which plant has more predictable output

Plant A's SD of 10 means most output is between 490-510 units, while Plant B's IQR of 50 shows the middle 50% spans a 50-unit range. Plant A is far more predictable.

A psychologist compares anxiety scores for two groups: therapy patients and a control group. The therapy group has a symmetric distribution centered at 35 with SD 5. The control group is right-skewed with a median of 55 and IQR of 15. What conclusions can be drawn

The therapy group has a lower center (35 vs 55) and smaller spread (SD 5 vs IQR 15), suggesting lower and more consistent anxiety levels compared to the control group.
